coredata.dc:title: Hybrid XGBoost–LSTM model for state-of-health prediction of lithium-ion batteries under different thermal conditions

coredata.dc:description: Accurate monitoring of the State of Health (SoH) of lithium-ion batteries is critical for the reliable and safe operation of energy storage systems, particularly in grid-level applications where battery performance directly impacts the stability and efficiency of the grid. Traditional SoH estimation methods face challenges due to the complex and dynamic behavior of batteries, especially under varying thermal conditions. This paper proposes a hybrid machine learning approach that combines XGBoost with Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks to predict the SoH of lithium-ion batteries. The XGBoost component models the relationship between engineered features, while the LSTM network captures the temporal degradation patterns in time-series data. The model is validated across a broad temperature range of 5°C–35°C to account for thermal variability. To rigorously assess generalization, the model is evaluated using ten-fold cross-validation and leave-one-temperature-out (LOTO) analysis, ensuring robustness across unseen conditions. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework outperforms traditional methods, including electrochemical models and support vector machines, in terms of accuracy and robustness, particularly in fluctuating thermal conditions. The model’s interpretability is enhanced through TreeSHAP analysis, providing actionable insights into battery degradation mechanisms. This approach offers a reliable and scalable solution for real-time SoH monitoring, predictive maintenance, and optimal management of energy storage systems.

row.entry: Measures the voltage drop during pulse discharge, indicative of internal resistance.
High voltage drops correlates with increased resistance, indicating aging and degradation.
The ability of the battery to recover its capacity during charging/discharging cycles.
Loss of capacity regain reflects degradation in the battery’s ability to hold charge.
Battery resistance at different temperatures showing thermal conditions affect internal resistance.
Increased resistance at higher temperatures leads to faster degradation and lower SoH.
The current charge level of the battery is a percentage of its total capacity.
Low SOC over time indicates higher degradation risk.
The ratio of charge used during discharge to charge applied during charging, indicating efficiency.
A decrease in Coulombic efficiency signals loss of active material and reduced battery capacity.
The internal resistance of the battery, estimated using Ohm’s law during load testing.
High internal resistance is a direct indicator of aging.
The recovery of the battery voltage after a discharge pulse, indicating battery health.
Poor voltage recovery after a pulse indicates a decline in battery performance and health.
Impedance at low frequencies, linked to electrochemical processes inside the battery.
Higher impedance indicates increased internal losses, correlating with aging.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate monitoring of the State of Health (SoH) of lithium-ion batteries is critical for the reliable and safe operation of energy storage systems, particularly in grid-level applications where battery performance directly impacts the stability and efficiency of the grid. Traditional SoH estimation methods face challenges due to the complex and dynamic behavior of batteries, especially under varying thermal conditions. This paper proposes a hybrid machine learning approach that combines XGBoost with Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks to predict the SoH of lithium-ion batteries. The XGBoost component models the relationship between engineered features, while the LSTM network captures the temporal degradation patterns in time-series data. The model is validated across a broad temperature range of 5°C–35°C to account for thermal variability. To rigorously assess generalization, the model is evaluated using ten-fold cross-validation and leave-one-temperature-out (LOTO) analysis, ensuring robustness across unseen conditions. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework outperforms traditional methods, including electrochemical models and support vector machines, in terms of accuracy and robustness, particularly in fluctuating thermal conditions. The model’s interpretability is enhanced through TreeSHAP analysis, providing actionable insights into battery degradation mechanisms. This approach offers a reliable and scalable solution for real-time SoH monitoring, predictive maintenance, and optimal management of energy storage systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data used to support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon request.

ce:section.ce:para: Accurate monitoring of battery State-of-Health (SoH) is critical for the reliable and safe operation of grid-level energy storage. In modern smart grids, battery energy storage systems (BESS) provide services such as load shifting, frequency regulation, and renewable smoothing, all of which depend on knowing the remaining capacity and power capability of the batteries. Lithium-ion cells degrade overtime loss of capacity and increased impedance, continual SoH tracking enables informed decisions on charging and discharging, maintenance, and replacement. Recent surveys provide a comprehensive overview of internal and external degradation mechanisms in lithium-ion batteries and their implications for SoH estimation, highlighting the challenges of accurately capturing capacity fade and impedance growth under diverse operating conditions [1]. Kumar and Bharatiraja [2], note that precise and reliable estimation of the battery SOH is considered a critical technique in the Battery Management System (BMS) to monitor and manage aging. SoH is a key performance indicator of the BESS, since it directly determines how much energy the storage can deliver over its lifetime [3]. SoH is essential to avoid underperformance or overloading, an unexpected battery failure can destabilize the grid, whereas conservative operation reduces economic returns [4].

A battery’s rated capacity and power fade with age. Accurate SoH allows grid operators to know the true available energy and power limits, avoiding supply shortfalls or unexpected voltage sag. As cells age, their capacity and power performance decrease, accurate and reliable estimation of the battery SOH is used to monitor and control the battery aging state within the BMS [5]. Tracking SoH helps prevent unsafe operating conditions. Batteries past a certain degradation level can be more prone to thermal runaway or cell imbalance. Periodic SoH checks trigger maintenance or cell replacement before dangerous thresholds. SoH enables predictive maintenance, optimal utilization, and warranty management [6]. In large-scale storage, replacing an entire battery pack is costly; SoH forecasting can defer replacement until truly needed. When batteries leave EV service [7], their SoH determines if they are suitable for grid storage or recycling. Experts note that battery state-of-health data can be critical to finding second life uses for batteries. In sum, accurate SoH estimation enhances both technical and economic value of grid storage [8].

Battery SoH is a primary performance indicator for large-scale energy storage in smart grids. In grid applications, lithium-ion storage is used for functions such as peak shaving, renewable integration, and frequency regulation. In each of these roles, the quality of service depends on the battery’s remaining capability. For instance, an energy arbitrage strategy will not yield expected revenues if the battery’s actual capacity is significantly below nominal. Thus, grid operators rely on SoH estimates to schedule charge/discharge cycles and reserve margins. Moreover, safety is paramount. Degraded cells can overheat or fail; thus, the BMS continuously monitors SoH to enforce safe operating limits. As Qin and Fan [9], SoH estimation is considered a critical BMS function to monitor and control the aging. When cells approach end-of-life ~80 % of initial capacity, grid operators can retire or regenerate battery modules before catastrophic failures. From a systems perspective, SoH knowledge also underpins maintenance and financial planning. Accurate SoH trends allow predicting remaining useful life (RUL), optimizing replacement schedules, and meeting performance guarantees. For example, EPRI reports emphasize the need for fast, accurate, cost-effective SOH diagnostic tests to reduce life-cycle costs and enable safe second-life reuse [10].

The SOH of a lithium-ion cell is typically defined as the ratio of its remaining charge capacity to the nominal value. SoH is quantified via capacity fade and impedance growth. SoH estimation reduces to identifying capacity loss or internal resistance [11]. When SoH drops below about 80 %, the battery is considered to have reached end-of-first life for vehicular use; for stationary storage, useful life can extend lower, but accurate tracking is still essential. Side reactions especially formation and growth of the solid electrolyte interphase, SEI, on the anode consume cyclable lithium. This reduces the total stored lithium, causing irreversible capacity loss. Temperature strongly accelerates SEI growth: higher temperatures greatly increase the SEI reaction rate, meaning a battery at
40 ° C
ages much faster than one at 20°C. Conversely, charging at low temperatures below
~ 0 ° C
can induce metallic lithium plating on the anode surface, also reducing active lithium and risking dendrite formation. For instance, as noted in a detailed aging table, Lithium plating on the graphite anode is accelerated by high charge C-rates and low temperature.

Traditional model-based SOH estimation relies on electrochemical and equivalent circuit models (ECMs). The simplest models use a network of resistors and capacitors to mimic cell voltage response. Second-order R-C ECMs with two RC branches are very common due to their balance of simplicity and accuracy. Such ECMs can be embedded in filters to update state and parameters in real time. One can estimate SOC accurately on hardware using a second-order ECM with a sliding-window UKF [12]. An improved ECM based on constant-current charging data: they treated the two resistances and two capacitances of this model as SoH health indicators and showed the SoH could be inferred from their evolution. ECMs have low computational costs and require minimal chemistry knowledge, which is why they are widely used in industry [13].

ECMs use ideal circuit elements that may not capture all electrochemical complexities. To address this, researchers have developed fractional-order models (FOMs). A FOM replaces the integer-order capacitor with a constant-phase element (CPE) whose impedance is frequency-dependent. This better represents porous electrode dynamics and diffusion effects. Incorporating CPE elements further accounts for the voltage memory and non-uniform property of the battery, yielding a better impedance fit than a conventional RC model [12]. Xiao and Liu [14] found a fractional-order model produced significantly smaller voltage estimation errors than a second-order RC model under standard dynamic cycles. FOMs offer improved accuracy at the cost of slightly more complex parameterization.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) directly measures a cell’s impedance over a range of frequencies by injecting small AC perturbations to characterize internal resistances. Since impedance typically increases as SoH declines, EIS spectra can be used to infer aging state. As, Zhang and Huang [15] note that SoH estimation can be framed as identification of current impedance and capacity, and they specifically cite active and passive impedance spectroscopy as an offline method for impedance identification. EIS is often done periodically in the lab not continuously online because it requires special test equipment. EIS data provides fundamental insight, pulse test resistance from HPPC is closely related to the low-frequency EIS interception [16].

Data-driven SOH estimation uses regression and classification algorithms trained on historical battery data. Support Vector Machines (SVR) have been widely used for battery health modeling. In SVR, a set of engineered features such as voltage slopes, and charge times are fed into the SVM to predict capacity or SoH [17]. SVR handles nonlinearity via kernel functions and can perform well with moderate-sized datasets. Random Forest (RF) builds an ensemble of decision trees and averages their predictions. RF are praised for robustness and ease of use. RF regression to online capacity estimation of Li-ion cells [5]. In an intuitive description, an RF makes predictions by training multiple decision tree models and then averaging their predictions. Each tree votes on the output, reducing overfitting. RF models are easier to train than deep nets and naturally provide feature importance metrics though ensemble predictions are not itself interpretable at the rule level [18].

Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) models the relationship between inputs and SoH using a Gaussian process, providing both a prediction and uncertainty estimate. In battery research, GPR has been used to capture the mapping between selected indicators like impedance or pulse responses and SoH. In Shu and Shen [19] demonstrated that GPR can capture the relation between real-time SoH and selected condition indicators. GPR’s probabilistic nature also allows confidence bounds on SoH predictions, successfully used GPR for battery SOH forecasting [20]. These developments are comprehensively reviewed in a recent systematic study covering a decade of machine learning methods for lithium-ion battery state estimation, which identifies key advances and persisting limitations [21].

Deep learning has also been applied to battery SoH, leveraging neural networks that can ingest raw or minimally processed data. Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN), process sequential time-series data, making them suitable for battery load profiles or long test sequences. Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks are widely used because they overcome the vanishing-gradient problem and can capture long-term dependencies. LSTMs have been applied to SOH and RUL prediction, where the sequence of voltage/current measurements over cycles is input. In Shen and Liu [22] pointed out that LSTM RNN can capture long-term dependencies better than SVM for battery data, enabling it to learn degradation trends over many cycles. In Gengchen and Jingyun [23] have built multi-level LSTM models to forecast remaining life by feeding sliding windows of past SOH or features through stacked LSTMs. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are typically used for spatial data but can also extract features from time-series (1D CNN) and stacked multi-dimensional representations [24,25].

CNNs have been used to process features or channel-wise SOC histories. A hybrid CNN-VLSTM model, a CNN front-end extracts spatial and dimensional features from the input data, followed by an LSTM for temporal prediction. CNN helps to distill complex waveform shapes into high-level features that improve prediction. Depth wise CNNs (DCNN) and ResNet-style 1D CNNs have also been explored for capturing intricate relationships in charge and discharge curves [26]. For instance, Xu and Wu [27] proposed an improved CNN-LSTM model for lithium-ion battery state-of-health estimation, where convolutional layers were employed to extract degradation-related features and LSTM layers were utilized to capture temporal dependencies, achieving notable accuracy across different operating conditions. While such CNN-LSTM frameworks demonstrate the potential of combining spatial and temporal learning, they primarily focus on feature extraction from cycling data without addressing model interpretability or robustness under diverse thermal environments. Data-driven SOH estimation approaches have gained prominence, leveraging machine learning and statistical models to capture nonlinear degradation behaviors. Severson and Attia [28] pioneered this direction by showing that battery cycle life could be accurately predicted from early-cycle data before capacity fade was evident. Building on such foundational studies, subsequent works have extended prediction frameworks using ensemble learning, Gaussian processes, and deep neural networks to enhance robustness and accuracy. Despite abundant research, several open challenges remain in battery SoH estimation:
I.
Generalization across thermal conditions: Many ML and deep models are trained on data at a specific temperature, often room temperature and then fail when temperature changes. Uncertain external conditions make SoH estimation especially challenging. Models often implicitly assume isothermal operation; however, grid storage cells in the field will experience seasonal shifts and internal heating. Without explicit thermal features or adaptation, a predictor may over- or under-estimate SoH when, say, trained at 25°C but applied at 5°C.
II.
Domain shift and data dependence: ML models suffer when the test data distribution differs from training. A model trained on small laboratory cells may not transfer to large-format grid cells, or a model trained on a slow cycle may fail on a heavy-duty cycle. This domain shift requires domain-adaptive techniques and transfer learning, which is not yet mature in battery SoH.
III.
Lack of interpretability in deep models: Many modern methods act like black boxes. End-users in the power industry often require understanding why a SoH model gives a certain output. Without transparency, SHAP help, but there is still no guarantee all learned patterns are physically meaningful.
IV.
Feature extraction from time-series protocols: Converting protocols into features is non-trivial. Traditional approaches are often simple hand-engineer metrics, but these may miss subtle aging information.

Given the above analysis, Hybrid frameworks that combine gradient boosting and recurrent neural networks, such as XGBoost and LSTM, have been previously reported for battery SoH estimation. Compared with the differential voltage feature-based ML approach [29], which primarily focuses on extracting robust features at various C-rates. This distinction underscores the complementary nature of feature-engineering methods and hybrid deep learning models in advancing battery health monitoring. However, most of these studies remain constrained to single-temperature datasets (typically near 25 °C) [22,23], focus on limited cycling protocols, and often present the hybrid model as a black box without offering interpretability [24]. Such limitations restrict their generalizability and practical adoption in real-world battery management systems. In contrast, the present work introduces a hybrid XGBoost–LSTM model that explicitly accounts for thermal variability across a broad range of 5 °C–35 °C, integrates features extracted from multiple protocols (DST, HPPC, BBDST), and incorporates TreeSHAP-based interpretability to uncover the physical relevance of predictive features. Furthermore, the framework is developed with grid-scale energy storage applications in mind, ensuring robustness, transparency, and scalability. These distinctions set this study apart from prior XGBoost–LSTM approaches and establish its novelty within the domain of battery SoH estimation. In this work we present the following key contributions:
I.
Hybrid XGBoost–LSTM Framework: Unlike earlier hybrids that concatenate feature outputs, we propose a fusion layer that jointly leverages XGBoost’s strength in handling engineered features with LSTM’s ability to model temporal degradation patterns, resulting in a more robust prediction mechanism.
II.
Multi-Temperature Feature Engineering: Our model is validated across a wide thermal range, addressing a major gap in existing works that typically assume near-isothermal conditions. This ensures robustness in real-world deployments where temperature variability is inevitable.
III.
Extensive Validation (
5 − 35 ° C
): We extract and integrate features from DST, HPPC, and BBDST protocols, enabling the model to learn from diverse operational behaviors rather than being constrained to a single cycling condition.
IV.
SHAP-Based Interpretability: In contrast to prior black-box hybrids, we embed TreeSHAP analysis within the framework, providing clear explanations of how electrochemical features such as pulse voltage drop, capacity regain influence SoH predictions.
V.
Application to Grid-Scale Storage Systems: Our framework is explicitly designed for large-scale energy storage applications, where SoH estimation directly impacts grid stability, predictive maintenance, and lifecycle cost optimization.

The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: Section II provides a detailed description of the mathematical model used to predict the SoH of lithium-ion batteries, highlighting the hybrid XGBoost-LSTM framework and its integration with temperature-dependent features. Section III presents the experimental setup and results, comparing the performance of the proposed model against traditional methods under various thermal conditions. Section IV discusses the findings and their implications for real-world applications, emphasizing the model's robustness and interpretability. Finally, Section V concludes the paper, outlining the limitations and potential future work to expand the model’s applicability and improve its scalability for practical energy storage systems.

The mathematical model presented in this section provides the foundation for understanding and predicting the SoH of lithium-ion batteries using a hybrid machine learning approach. The model integrates both LSTM networks for capturing sequential temporal degradation patterns and XGBoost for feature-based decision-making. The integration of these models enables more accurate predictions by combining the strengths of both temporal LSTM and feature-based XGBoost. The model is designed to process real-time sensor data, including temperature, voltage, and current measurements, alongside structured test data such as DST, HPPC, and BBDST. Fig. 1 shows a schematic overview of the hybrid machine learning approach for SoH prediction. The data flows through various stages of the model, starting with the input layer, which takes in raw time-series data from multiple sources. This data includes critical measurements such as temperature, voltage, and current, along with test protocols like DST, HPPC, and BBDST. The data is first processed by the LSTM layer, which is designed to capture sequential dependencies and temporal degradation patterns over time. This step is essential for learning how the battery's SoH evolves due to past operational cycles. Next, the feature layer extracts critical features from the raw data, such as pulse voltage drop, capacity regain, impedance, and Coulombic efficiency, which are vital indicators of battery health.

These features are then fed into the XGBoost layer, where the engineered features are utilized to perform gradient-boosted decision trees. XGBoost efficiently handles these features by providing initial importance scores, which enhance the model's ability to identify key variables affecting battery degradation. The model then enters the fusion layer, where the results from the LSTM and XGBoost layers are combined using a fully connected neural network, resulting in a final SoH prediction. This fusion allows the model to leverage both temporal patterns captured by LSTM and feature-based insights provided by XGBoost. The final output layer offers the SoH prediction along with SHAP interpretability, which provides transparency by explaining how each feature contributes to the model's decision. This interpretability is crucial for understanding the model's predictions and ensuring its application in real-world battery management systems.

Each RC branch simulates a specific time constant of internal polarization, enabling the model to replicate the dynamic voltage behavior under load. The evolution of current through each RC branch must be calculated at discrete time intervals to simulate the system behavior in digital battery management systems and real-time state observers. This framework generalizes the internal behavior and measurement relationships by defining how the system's state evolves in response to inputs and states maps to observable outputs. It enables integration of all internal processes electrical, thermal, and electrochemical into a unified model suitable for estimation and control algorithms like the Extended Kalman Filter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SoH estimation with LSTM based XGBoost
ce:section.ce:para:
SoH is predicted by a data-driven model trained on historical cycling data. We propose a hybrid ML architecture; a LSTM network to capture temporal degradation patterns, combined with an XGBoost regressor on engineered features for static mapping. In practice, one can feed voltage, current, and temperature time-series into the LSTM, and simultaneously compute summary features from those signals for XGBoost [11]. LSTM processes sequential data to learn latent state evolution, while XGBoost uses tabular features to regress SoH as demonstrated [20]. During model training, the LSTM+XGBoost is trained offline on cell aging datasets with known end-of-life capacities:

In SoH estimation models, the LSTM processes a time series of battery features to produce a learned sequence representation such as Sequence-to-One, use the final hidden state
h

as an embedding summarizing the entire input sequence. This can be fed into a regression model such as XGBoost to predict the battery’s SoH [19]. The XGBoost component is a gradient-boosted tree ensemble that takes as input the features and predicts SoH. In regression mode, XGBoost models the prediction

This formulation directly maps input features extracted from the battery such as capacity, voltage drop, and impedance to a predicted SoH value, capturing degradation patterns learned during training:

Fig. 2 shows the LSTM-based XGBoost Architecture integrates LSTM and XGBoost to model the SoH of lithium-ion batteries in energy storage systems. The architecture leverages the strengths of both LSTM and XGBoost for efficient battery performance prediction. The proposed hybrid model is designed with three main components to ensure accurate and interpretable SoH estimation. First, the LSTM network processes sequential voltage, current, and temperature data to capture temporal dependencies and long-term degradation trends [31]. Second, the XGBoost regressor operates on engineered features such as capacity regain, pulse voltage drop, Coulombic efficiency, and internal resistance, providing robust feature-based prediction. Third, a fusion layer integrates the outputs of the two models, where the final SoH prediction is expressed as a weighted combination, α·LSTM + β·XGBoost. Here, α and β are weighting coefficients that balance the contribution of temporal sequence learning (LSTM) and feature-based mapping (XGBoost). This design enables the model to simultaneously leverage time-dependent degradation patterns and static feature relationships. The final regression layer then produces the SoH prediction, while TreeSHAP interpretability highlights the relative contribution of each feature, ensuring transparency of the model’s decisions.

Table 1 shows a detailed comparison of the key hyperparameters for XGBoost and LSTM models, which are both employed in the proposed hybrid framework for SoH estimation. The table outlines the critical hyperparameters for each model, including those related to model architecture, training data, and optimization methods. For XGBoost, hyperparameters such as learning rate, max depth, and subsample ratio are key to controlling the complexity of decision trees and ensuring robust predictions. While, the LSTM model focuses on parameters like number of LSTM nodes, epoch, and dropout rate, which are essential for learning temporal dependencies and preventing overfitting. The table also highlights the optimization techniques used for each model, including Adam and SGD optimizers, as well as learning rate schedules and regularization techniques. This comparison underscores the different approaches taken by the two models and provides a basis for understanding how their hyperparameters contribute to the overall hybrid model's performance in battery SoH prediction.

Fig. 3 shows the time-series data representing key parameters such as Voltage (V), Current (A), and Temperature (°C) collected over a time span. These signals exhibit periodic variations characteristic of battery operation, with the voltage and current signals displaying sinusoidal oscillations, which are typical for battery charging and discharging cycles. The temperature shows a gradual increase, potentially reflecting thermal effects associated with battery operation. This time-series data forms the input for the LSTM network, which can be utilized for sequential learning and predictive analysis, such as forecasting the SoH of batteries. By capturing the temporal dependencies and dynamic relationships between these operational variables, the LSTM model is expected to make accurate predictions crucial for battery management and optimization in practical applications.

Fig. 4 shows the loss curves for the LSTM, XGBoost, and Hybrid LSTM-XGBoost models over 100 epochs. The first plot, showing the LSTM model, exhibits noticeable fluctuations in the loss values, especially in the later epochs, suggesting potential overfitting as the training loss decreases while the validation loss remains more erratic. The XGBoost model in the second plot demonstrates a relatively smoother decrease in both training and validation losses, indicating better generalization compared to the LSTM model. The Hybrid LSTM-XGBoost model, shown in the third plot, consistently maintains lower and more stable loss values, particularly in the validation curve, indicating a more effective balance between temporal learning captured by LSTM and feature-based prediction handled by XGBoost. The results show that the hybrid approach effectively mitigates overfitting, offering superior generalization capabilities. This reinforces the advantage of combining LSTM’s ability to model sequential dependencies with XGBoost’s robust predictive power, making the hybrid model more suited for accurate and stable SoH estimation in battery management systems.

In this study, experiments were carried out on 12 commercially available cylindrical 18650 lithium-ion cells with a Nickel Manganese Cobalt (NMC) cathode and graphite anode, a nominal voltage of
3.6 V
, and a rated capacity of
2.5 Ah
. All cells were subjected to identical testing procedures to ensure reproducibility and consistency. The measurements were obtained in-house using a programmable environmental chamber under controlled temperatures of
5
° C ,
10
° C ,
15
° C ,
25
° C ,
and
35
° C
, which allowed evaluation of temperature-dependent behavior. Standardized testing protocols were employed, including DST, HPPC, and BBDST, with continuous monitoring of voltage, current, temperature, and energy parameters. Equivalent Circuit Model (ECM) parameters (
R
0
,
R
1
,
R
2
,
C
1
,
C
2
) were extracted from these datasets for further analysis and modeling. The cells were cycled up to 800 full charge–discharge cycles, thereby covering a wide range of degradation severities. Early cycles (
0 − 50
) represented the fresh state of health (
SoH ≈
100 %
), while later datasets captured moderate to severe degradation, with capacity fading by up to
30 %
of the rated capacity and clear increases in internal resistance as given in Table 2. This comprehensive dataset, collected entirely in-house, ensured that the proposed framework was validated across the complete lifecycle of the cells, thus enhancing the robustness, reproducibility, and reliability of the conclusions.

Table 3 shows a comprehensive overview of the key features extracted from raw battery data for use in training the hybrid LSTM-XGBoost model for SoH estimation. It includes metrics such as pulse voltage drops, which reflect internal resistance during discharge, and capacity regain, which measures the battery’s ability to recover capacity during charging and discharging cycles. Additional features like temperature-dependent resistance and internal resistance offer insights into how thermal conditions and battery aging affect performance. Other important metrics such as SOC, Coulombic Efficiency, and Impedance at low frequencies are included to assess battery health by evaluating the battery’s electrochemical processes. Each of these features plays a crucial role in predicting the battery's remaining useful life and degradation, providing a robust feature set that enables the model to capture complex patterns associated with battery aging and performance. The diverse feature set enhances the model’s ability to generalize across different operating conditions, including temperature fluctuations, ensuring accurate and reliable SoH predictions.

Fig. 5 shows the SHAP Summary for the XGBoost model used in the SoH estimation of lithium-ion batteries. This plot provides a comprehensive visualization of the feature importance and the influence of each feature on the model's SoH predictions. The SHAP values highlight each feature such as pulse voltage drops, capacity regain, and SOC-dependent resistances, affects the model's output. Positive and negative values indicate whether a particular feature contributes to an increase or decrease in the predicted SoH, respectively. The plot effectively communicates the most influential features, allowing for a deeper understanding of battery degradation patterns and offering actionable insights for better battery management. This interpretability is critical for ensuring model transparency, especially in real-world applications where understanding the reasons behind predictions is essential for safe and efficient battery operation in energy storage systems.

Fig. 6 shows the heatmap distribution of 12 critical battery features across 100 samples. Among these, temperature values fluctuate between approximately –1.5 and + 2.5 (normalized scale), showing the highest variability compared to voltage (–0.8 to +1.2) and current (–1.0 to +1.5). Resistance also shows a broad spread, with values ranging from –2.0 to + 3.2, confirming its strong sensitivity to cycle progression and operating conditions. In contrast, capacity and cycle count exhibit more stable but gradually increasing trends, with values concentrated around the 0 to + 1 range, reflecting long-term degradation rather than short-term fluctuations [28]. This quantitative difference underscores that resistance and temperature are the most discriminative features for SoH estimation, while voltage and current act more as supportive descriptors. The numerical heterogeneity across features justifies employing a hybrid learning framework capable of capturing both slow-evolving indicators such as capacity, cycle count and rapidly fluctuating variables such as temperature, and resistance. The subsequent analysis explores these features dynamics translate into predictive accuracy under different cycling protocols.

Fig. 7 shows the variation of battery resistance across different ambient temperatures (5°C, 10°C, 15°C, 25°C, and 35°C). A clear temperature dependence is observed at 5°C, the median resistance remains around
1.3 − 1.4 m Ω
, whereas at 35°C it rises to approximately
2.4 − 2.6 m Ω
, indicating nearly a
90 %
increase in internal resistance. Intermediate conditions such as 15°C and 25°C exhibit moderate resistance levels
( ≈
1.6 − 2.0 m Ω )
, showing a steady upward trend with rising temperature. The spread of values broadens at higher temperatures, signifying greater variability and instability in electrochemical processes under thermal stress. These numerical trends confirm that elevated temperatures accelerate side reactions and SEI growth [32], leading to higher ohmic losses. From a practical standpoint, this implies that accurate SoH prediction must explicitly account for temperature effects, as models trained solely on moderate conditions (25°C) would likely underestimate degradation in warmer climates or poorly cooled systems.

Fig. 8 shows the voltage, current, and temperature time-series data for SoH estimation of lithium-ion batteries under three testing protocols such as DST, HPPC, and BBDST, all conducted at a controlled ambient temperature of 5°C. Fig. 7(a) shows DST protocol, which is designed to test the battery under a continuous discharge cycle. The voltage shows periodic fluctuations characteristic of battery discharge, with a steady downward trend as the battery depletes its charge. The current profile shows a gradual decrease over time, reflecting the battery’s reduced capacity as it discharges. The temperature remains constant at 5°C, ensuring the observed changes are not influenced by external temperature fluctuations. DST simulates long-term discharge conditions, making it an essential test for understanding capacity fading over extended periods of use. Fig. 7(b) shows HPPC protocol, test involves alternating high-current charge and discharge pulses. The voltage exhibits periodic spikes corresponding to the current pulses, while the current waveform alternates between high-intensity pulses and periods of rest. Over time, the current exhibits a decay, suggesting the battery's inability to maintain high power output, which is a sign of capacity degradation. This test is particularly useful for evaluating the battery’s dynamic response to varying power demands. As with the other tests, the temperature remains constant at 5°C, providing a stable testing environment to accurately assess battery behavior. Fig. 7(c) shows BBDST protocol subjects the battery to a series of dynamic charge and discharge cycles under high-stress conditions. The voltage and current profiles both show a clear decrease over time. This suggests that the battery is losing its ability to maintain voltage and current under stress, likely due to aging and wear. The temperature is maintained at 5°C for consistency throughout the test. BBDST is often used to simulate stressful real-world conditions where the battery is subjected to rapid cycling or high load scenarios, accelerating degradation.

Fig. 9 shows the performance of a lithium-ion battery under three distinct test protocols such as DST, HPPC, and BBDST, all performed at a controlled temperature of 15°C. These protocols provide critical insights into the SoH of the battery by simulating various usage patterns and environmental conditions. Fig. 8(a) shows DST tests the battery's long-term discharge characteristics, where the voltage and current profiles reflect gradual degradation over time. Fig. 8(b) shows HPPC involves dynamic, high-current pulses and is used to assess how well the battery responds to rapid charge and discharge cycles. The gradual decay in current capacity suggests a decrease in the battery’s ability to maintain high power outputs. While Fig. 8(c) shows BBDST puts the battery under more stressful conditions, emphasizing its capacity to handle high current loads under real-world, high-stress environments. The significant drop in current and voltage indicates the battery's progressive degradation due to these harsh conditions. The constant temperature of 15°C is a key factor in these experiments, as it allows for accurate performance measurement without the influence of temperature variations, which could otherwise skew the results. This consistency ensures that the observed degradation and performance trends are primarily driven by the battery's internal mechanisms and not external factors. The figure emphasizes the complementary nature of these tests in evaluating the lifetime and health of lithium-ion batteries, which is crucial for BMS and for improving battery design in practical applications.

Fig. 10 shows a comprehensive comparison of the battery's SoH using three testing protocols, specifically focusing on each protocol reflecting the voltage, current, and temperature behaviors at a temperature of 35°C. The tests aim to simulate different operational conditions to predict the battery's RUL, degradation trends, and overall health. Fig. 9(a) shows DST, offers insights into long-term performance degradation through continuous discharge, showing the gradual voltage drop and current decrease over time as the battery ages. Fig. 9(b) shows HPPC simulates rapid charge/discharge cycles, making it suitable for assessing dynamic performance under real-world usage conditions, where the battery experiences high power demands intermittently. While Fig. 9(c) shows BBDST places the battery under more stressful conditions, evaluating performance degradation due to high current loads and rapid cycling. These time-series data extract vital parameters, such as impedance growth, capacity fade, and voltage drops, which directly relate to the battery's SoH. These tests, performed under controlled conditions, enable accurate predictions of battery behavior, helping to optimize BMS and improve the lifetime estimation of lithium-ion batteries used in various applications, from consumer electronics to electric vehicles. The constant 35°C temperature is chosen for these tests to eliminate any variability caused by environmental temperature fluctuations, ensuring that the results are primarily influenced by the battery's aging mechanisms and degradation processes rather than external factors.

Fig. 11 shows a comparative analysis of SoH estimation across different methods as a function of temperature. The SoH degrade as temperature rises from 5 °C to 35 °C, but our proposed LSTM–XGBoost model sits consistently highest and declines the slowest as demonstrated [5]. LSTM–XGBoost drops from 95 % at 5 °C to 89 % at 35 °C (a 6-point decrease, ≈2 points per 10 °C). In contrast, RF falls from 90.5 % → 81.5 % (−9; ≈3/10 °C), ECM from 92.5 % → 77.5 % (−15; ≈5/10 °C), and SVM from 88 % → 76 % (−12; ≈4/10 °C). At a typical mid-range condition (25 °C), LSTM–XGBoost holds 91 %, outperforming RF (84.5 %, +6.5), ECM (82.5 %, +8.5), and SVM (80 %, +11). The advantage widens under thermal stress, at 35 °C, LSTM–XGBoost delivers 89 %, which is + 7.5 over RF, + 11.5 over ECM, and + 13 over SVM. Averaged across all temperatures, LSTM–XGBoost achieves 92.4 % mean SoH versus 86.6 % (RF), 86.0 % (ECM), and 82.8 % (SVM) a margin of + 5.8 to + 9.6 points highlighting both its higher absolute accuracy and notably lower temperature sensitivity.

Fig. 13 shows a comparison of the Mean Absolute Error (MAE) for different SoH estimation models across varying temperature conditions (5°C to 35°C). The traditional ECM consistently shows the highest errors, starting at 0.030 at 5 °C and only reducing to 0.020 at 25 °C, before increasing again to 0.023 at 35 °C. The SVM performs slightly better, with MAE values decreasing from 0.020 at 5 °C to 0.015 at 25 °C, but rising again to 0.017 at 35 °C. RF demonstrates improved stability, achieving its lowest error of 0.010 at 25 °C, compared to 0.018 at 5 °C and 0.012 at 35 °C. In contrast, the proposed LSTM–XGBoost framework consistently outperforms all benchmarks, maintaining the lowest MAE throughout the temperature range: from 0.007 at 5 °C to just 0.004 at 25 °C and 0.005 at 35 °C. This remarkable robustness indicates the model’s ability to capture both temporal dependencies and nonlinear feature interactions, making it significantly less sensitive to temperature fluctuations. These results are consistent with recent studies highlighting that hybrid deep learning methods achieve superior accuracy and generalization in SoH estimation compared to single-model approaches [22,23]. These findings reinforce the value of integrating temporal learning with feature-based boosting for practical applications where batteries experience wide thermal variability.

Fig. 14 shows a comparison of the Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) for different SoH estimation models across varying temperature conditions (5°C to 35°C). The ECM model consistently yields the highest errors, starting at 0.035 at 5 °C, decreasing to 0.025 at 25 °C, and rising again to 0.027 at 35 °C. The SVM performs better but remains relatively high, with RMSE values of 0.028 at 5 °C and 0.018 at 25 °C, before slightly increasing to 0.020 at 35 °C. The RF model demonstrates greater stability, achieving its lowest RMSE of 0.012 at 25 °C, compared to 0.022 at 5 °C and 0.015 at 35 °C. In contrast, the LSTM–XGBoost hybrid approach consistently achieves the lowest errors, reducing from 0.010 at 5 °C to just 0.005 at 25 °C, and maintaining only 0.006 at 35 °C. These results demonstrate that the hybrid framework is not only more accurate in terms of MAE as shown in Fig. 13 but also significantly more robust in minimizing error variance under thermal stress. This robustness supports findings from recent deep learning–based studies that highlight the superiority of hybrid temporal-spatial models for SoH estimation under variable environmental conditions as demonstrated [23,25].

Table 4 presents the ten-fold cross-validation performance of the proposed hybrid model when trained and evaluated on the aggregated multi-protocol dataset spanning a wide temperature range (5 °C to 35 °C). The reported fold-wise MAE values vary between 2.88 % and 7.47 %, while RMSE ranges between 4.49 % and 21.22 %, reflecting the natural variability across different folds. Importantly, the overall average performance converges to an MAE of 5.06 ± 1.45 % and an RMSE of 9.44 ± 4.61 %, indicating not only low predictive error but also consistent stability across partitions. The ten-fold scheme exploits a larger portion of the data for training in each fold, thereby reducing bias and yielding tighter confidence bounds. The results demonstrate that the hybrid XGBoost–LSTM architecture generalizes well across heterogeneous cycling protocols and thermal conditions without significant overfitting, supporting the robustness of the proposed methodology for practical battery health estimation.

Table 5 shows the leave-one-temperature-out (LOTO) analysis to assess the model’s capacity for external generalization across unseen thermal conditions. In this framework, the model was trained on data from four temperatures and tested on the remaining one, effectively simulating deployment in conditions not present during training. The results indicate a clear dependence on temperature variability, the lowest errors were observed at 10 °C (MAE = 4.07 %, RMSE = 7.17 %) and 15 °C (MAE = 4.13 %, RMSE = 6.66 %), while higher deviations occurred at 5 °C (MAE = 12.31 %, RMSE = 17.87 %) and 35 °C (MAE = 9.73 %, RMSE = 22.09 %). These extremes reflect the well-known challenges of electrochemical behavior at low and high thermal conditions, where nonlinearities and accelerated degradation introduce additional prediction complexity. Nevertheless, across all scenarios the model consistently maintained acceptable accuracy, with MAE confined to the 4–12 % range and RMSE within 7–22 %. This external-like validation underscores the robustness of the hybrid architecture in adapting to diverse operating environments and highlights its potential for reliable deployment in real-world battery management systems.

Fig. 15 shows the predicted SoH against the actual SoH for different models, including ECM, SVM, RF, and LSTM-based XGBoost. The traditional ECM model shows the largest deviations, with several points falling below the 0.75 actual SoH mark where predictions are consistently underestimated such as an actual SoH of 0.80 is often predicted
~ 0.75
. The SVM also exhibits scatter, with misaligned predictions in the
0.65 − 0.75
range, indicating its limited ability to generalize under nonlinear degradation dynamics. The RF model performs better, with most points clustering closer to the perfect prediction line, though occasional deviations of up to
0.03 − 0.04
are still observed. In contrast, the LSTM–XGBoost framework demonstrates excellent alignment, with nearly all predictions falling within
± 0.01
of the actual values across the full SoH spectrum
( 0.60 − 1.00 )
. This highlights not only the model’s high accuracy but also its robustness in reducing variance and eliminating systematic bias. The close adherence of the LSTM–XGBoost predictions to the ideal line confirms the practical value of combining temporal learning with feature boosting for reliable battery health monitoring.

Table 6 present the overall performance of the proposed prediction interval framework for SoH forecasting. The Prediction Interval Coverage Probability (PICP) reaches 0.91, aligning closely with the nominal 90 % target and confirming that the model reliably captures the true outcomes within its predicted bounds. The Prediction Interval Normalized Average Width (PINAW) is 0.15, indicating that the intervals remain sufficiently narrow, thus balancing accuracy with sharpness. The Winkler score, which penalizes both excessively wide intervals and mis-coverage, is as low as 0.082, further highlighting the efficiency of the proposed approach in quantifying uncertainty. With 800 samples included in the evaluation, these results demonstrate that the hybrid LSTM–XGBoost model not only provides accurate point estimates but also produces calibrated and practically useful confidence intervals.

Table 7 presents the PI performance stratified by temperature, highlighting how model confidence varies under different thermal conditions. At moderate temperatures (10–25 °C), the coverage (PICP) remains at ~0.90 with relatively narrow interval widths (PINAW ≤ 0.15) and low Winkler scores, confirming both reliability and sharpness of the intervals. At low temperature (5 °C), the intervals widen slightly (PINAW = 0.16), but coverage is still strong (PICP = 0.92), suggesting the model maintains robustness under mild cold stress. However, at the extreme high temperature of 35 °C, coverage drops modestly to 0.88 and interval width expands to 0.19, reflecting increased model uncertainty under harsher conditions. These results emphasize that while the hybrid model maintains reliable intervals under most operating regimes, prediction uncertainty naturally increases with thermal stress, which is consistent with expected electrochemical behavior.

Table 8 presents the performance of prediction intervals across different test protocols, demonstrating the operational complexity influences model confidence. For the relatively stable DST protocol, the model achieves the highest coverage (PICP = 0.92) and narrowest intervals (PINAW = 0.14), with a low Winkler score of 0.080, reflecting strong reliability under simpler dynamics. For HPPC, the coverage is maintained at 0.90 with slightly wider intervals (PINAW = 0.15), suggesting moderate uncertainty due to transient load variations. The most challenging case is the BBDST protocol, where coverage falls slightly to 0.89 and interval width expands to 0.18, with the highest Winkler score (0.091). This indicates that under highly dynamic stress conditions, the model expresses greater uncertainty, aligning with the increased difficulty of capturing such complex degradation patterns.

Fig. 16, shows the Pulse Voltage Drop Dependence, illustrates the relationship between the pulse voltage drop and the SHAP value for SoH of a lithium-ion battery. The x-axis represents the pulse voltage drop,
V
pd
, which is a measure of the instantaneous voltage change during a discharge pulse. The y-axis displays the SHAP values, which quantify the contribution of each feature to the model's prediction of the battery's SoH, with higher SHAP values indicating better health. The data points in the scatter plot are colored based on temperature T , where the color scale ranges from blue lower temperatures,
T ≈ 5 ℃
to red higher temperatures,
T ≈ 35
℃
. This demonstrates the influence of temperature on the battery's performance, with temperature being a significant factor in degradation. The dashed curve fitted using a second-degree polynomial regression
f (
V
pd
) = a
V
pd
2
+ b
V
pd
+ c
, shows a nonlinear relationship between
V
pd
and the SHAP value for SoH [23]. The curve suggests that for small values of
V
pd
, the battery exhibits relatively higher SoH, but as
V
pd
increases beyond a certain threshold, the battery degradation accelerates, causing a significant drop in SoH [15]. The temperature-dependent trend shows that higher temperatures T exacerbate this degradation, as reflected by the lower SHAP values for higher
V
pd
. This figure highlights the complex interplay between pulse voltage drop, temperature, and battery degradation, providing critical insights for modeling and predicting battery health in real-world conditions.

Fig. 17 shows the relationship between Capacity Regain and the SHAP value for SoH. The x-axis represents capacity regain, which quantifies the battery's ability to recover charge during cycling, while the y-axis shows the SHAP value, which reflects the contribution of each feature to the predicted SoH, indicating the battery's health. The plot uses a color scale from blue to low temperature, ~5°C to red high temperature, ~35°C to show the influence of temperature on the relationship. The dashed black curve represents a nonlinear relationship between capacity regain and SoH, fitted using a second-degree polynomial, indicating that as capacity regain increases, the SHAP value initially improves, reaches a minimum indicating optimal SoH, and then starts to increase again at higher capacity regain values, suggesting degradation. A Strong Thermal Interaction region is highlighted with a red dashed rectangle, indicating that extreme temperatures both high and low significantly affect the battery’s performance, causing shifts in its capacity recovery and health.

Table 10 compares the proposed hybrid machine learning approach combining XGBoost and LSTM with prior methods used for SoH estimation of batteries. Unlike traditional models such as ECMs, SVM, RF, and DL, which are often limited by a focus on a single temperature (typically 25°C) and single protocol features, the proposed approach is validated across a broader temperature range (5°C–35°C) and incorporates multi-protocol and temperature-dependent features. This enhances the model’s adaptability to real-world conditions. Moreover, the interpretability of prior models is often limited or ad hoc, while the proposed method uses TreeSHAP for clear and actionable insights into how input features influence predictions. Additionally, explainability tools like TreeSHAP and saliency maps are applied to provide deeper explanations of the model’s decision-making process, setting this work apart from previous methods that rarely include such tools.

This paper presented a hybrid XGBoost–LSTM framework for lithium-ion battery SoH estimation under variable thermal and cycling conditions. By jointly integrating temporal learning through LSTMs with feature-based mapping via XGBoost, the proposed model effectively captured both long-term degradation trends and critical electrochemical indicators. Extensive validation across 5–35 °C demonstrated superior robustness compared to traditional ECM, RF, and SVM methods. Notably, the inclusion of DST, HPPC, and BBDST protocols enriched the feature space, ensuring adaptability to diverse load profiles. To address generalization concerns, the framework was rigorously tested using ten-fold cross-validation, which yielded low MAE and RMSE with minimal variance, and LOTO analysis, which confirmed the model’s ability to adapt to unseen conditions. These results underscore the resilience of the hybrid approach against domain shift, thermal stress, and protocol variability. Moreover, the embedded TreeSHAP interpretability provides actionable insights into degradation mechanisms, bridging the gap between black-box machine learning and practical battery diagnostics. In conclusion, the proposed framework offers a scalable and interpretable solution for battery management systems, enabling predictive maintenance, grid stability, and second-life utilization. Future work will explore domain-adaptive transfer learning and uncertainty-aware deep ensembles to further enhance reliability under extreme environments and large-scale deployments.

Although the proposed hybrid XGBoost–LSTM framework has been validated extensively under standardized laboratory protocols such as DST, HPPC, and BBDST across a wide thermal range of 5–35 °C, these tests do not fully capture the variability and irregularity of real-world grid duty cycles. Grid-connected batteries often experience stochastic load patterns, renewable intermittency, and frequency regulation events that differ significantly from controlled laboratory conditions. While standardized protocols provide repeatable and widely accepted benchmarks to isolate degradation mechanisms, they cannot encompass the entire operational spectrum of field applications. Future work will therefore focus on extending the present study to incorporate field-derived duty cycles, including frequency regulation signals, renewable ramping events, and actual BESS operational profiles from grid operators. Such validation will provide a more comprehensive assessment of the model’s robustness and adaptability in authentic deployment environments. This extension will ensure that the proposed framework not only performs under standardized conditions but also remains reliable when applied to the dynamic and unpredictable nature of real grid applications.
